title: Nursing
chapterIntro: In nursing, clear Spanish communication saves lives. Whether you're explaining medication effects, instructing a patient on wound care, or preparing someone for discharge, precision and compassion are non-negotiable. This chapter teaches you the language of medication administration, vital signs monitoring, and patient education—the daily interactions that build trust and ensure safety.

grammarFocus.title: The Formal Imperative: Giving Instructions with Clarity and Respect
grammarFocus.explanation: Spanish nurses and doctors use the formal imperative (usted commands) constantly when instructing patients. Unlike English imperatives, Spanish formal commands follow specific conjugation patterns and sound more respectful than direct orders. Mastering this form is essential for medication instructions ('Tome dos pastillas'), wound care directions ('Limpie la herida'), and discharge teaching ('Guarde los medicamentos en un lugar frío'). The formal imperative conveys authority while maintaining the professional dignity patients expect in healthcare settings.

examples.en: Do not remove the bandage for 24 hours.
examples.es: No quite el vendaje durante 24 horas.

examples.en: Call us if you experience any side effects.
examples.es: Llame si experimenta algún efecto secundario.

extendedVocab.notes: Both terms are used; medicamento is slightly more formal in clinical settings.
Always specify: 'una dosis de 500 miligramos' (one 500 mg dose).
'Suero' is commonly used for IV fluids; 'gotero' for the drip apparatus.
'Lesión' (injury) is broader; 'herida' specifically means a cut or open wound.

extendedVocab.es: instrucciones de alta / instrucciones de egreso
extendedVocab.notes: 'Alta' (discharge) is standard in Latin America; 'egreso' is also used.

extendedVocab.es: extracción de sangre / análisis de sangre / toma de muestra
extendedVocab.notes: 'Análisis de sangre' emphasizes the test; 'extracción' emphasizes the procedure.
'Constantes vitales' is equally common in Spanish-speaking healthcare.

extendedVocab.es: sonda urinaria / catéter / sonda de Foley
extendedVocab.notes: 'Sonda' is the general term; Foley is recognized but Spanish uses 'sonda urinaria.'
'Efectos secundarios' is patient-friendly; 'reacciones adversas' is more clinical.

extendedVocab.es: nivel del dolor / escala del dolor
extendedVocab.notes: Always ask: '¿En una escala del 1 al 10, cuál es su dolor?' (On a scale of 1-10, what is your pain?)
'Náuseas' is plural; 'vómito' (singular) means actual vomiting; distinguish carefully.
'Tiene fiebre' (you have a fever) vs. 'Su temperatura es...' (your temperature is...).
Critical: '¿Tiene alguna alergia?' (Do you have any allergies?) should be asked every shift.
Common post-operative concerns; ask directly and without judgment.
'¿Ha dormido bien?' (Have you slept well?) is important for recovery assessment.
'Hinchazón' is more colloquial; 'inflamación' is more clinical.
'¿Cómo cicatriza la herida?' (How is the wound healing?)

extendedVocab.es: cita de seguimiento / control de seguimiento
extendedVocab.notes: Patients must understand when to return; be explicit with dates.

phrases.en: I'm going to give you this medication now.
phrases.es: Le voy a administrar este medicamento ahora.
phrases.context: Medication administration; use before any injection or oral medication.

phrases.en: Take this medication twice a day with meals.
phrases.es: Tome este medicamento dos veces al día con las comidas.

phrases.en: You may feel a slight burning or coldness when I inject this.
phrases.es: Puede sentir un ligero ardor o frío cuando le inyecte esto.
phrases.context: Setting expectations before IV injection or medication administration.

phrases.en: Keep this area clean and dry. Do not wet the bandage.
phrases.es: Mantenga esta área limpia y seca. No moje el vendaje.

phrases.en: Call us immediately if you experience chest pain, difficulty breathing, or severe dizziness.
phrases.es: Llame inmediatamente si experimenta dolor de pecho, dificultad para respirar o mareos graves.
phrases.context: Red flag symptoms; critical for discharge safety.

phrases.en: On a scale of 1 to 10, how would you rate your pain right now?
phrases.es: En una escala del 1 al 10, ¿cómo calificaría su dolor en este momento?
phrases.context: Pain assessment; used multiple times per shift.

phrases.en: I need to take your vital signs. This will only take a moment.
phrases.es: Necesito tomar sus signos vitales. Esto solo tomará un momento.

phrases.en: Have you had any nausea, vomiting, or loss of appetite since yesterday?
phrases.es: ¿Ha tenido náuseas, vómitos o pérdida de apetito desde ayer?

phrases.en: Please empty your bladder before we begin the procedure.
phrases.es: Por favor, vacíe su vejiga antes de que comencemos el procedimiento.

phrases.en: This catheter will help drain your urine. It may feel uncomfortable, but it is necessary.
phrases.es: Este catéter lo ayudará a drenar la orina. Puede sentirse incómodo, pero es necesario.

phrases.en: Do not remove this IV line yourself. If it comes out, notify a nurse immediately.
phrases.es: No quite esta línea intravenosa usted mismo. Si se sale, notifique a una enfermera inmediatamente.
phrases.context: Safety instruction for ambulatory patients with IVs.

phrases.en: When you go home, watch for signs of infection: increased redness, warmth, pus, or foul odor.
phrases.es: Cuando regrese a casa, observe signos de infección: enrojecimiento aumentado, calor, pus u olor desagradable.
phrases.context: Discharge teaching for wound care awareness.

exercises.title: Fill in the Blank: Medication Instructions
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct Spanish term from the word bank. These are instructions you will give patients daily.

items.prompt: La _____ de este medicamento es una tableta cada ocho horas.
Le voy a administrar este _____ a través de su suero.
Algunos _____ comunes incluyen mareos y náuseas.
Limpie la _____ con agua estéril dos veces al día.
Esta _____ le permite recibir fluidos y medicamentos directamente en la vena.

exercises.title: Quick Translation: Vital Nursing Phrases
exercises.instructions: Translate these essential nursing instructions into Spanish. Focus on the formal imperative (usted command form).

items.es: Reporte cualquier dolor de pecho inmediatamente.

items.en: Keep the wound covered until it heals.
items.es: Mantenga la herida cubierta hasta que cicatrice.

culturalNote: In many Spanish-speaking healthcare contexts, family presence during patient care is expected and valued—often a family member will stay overnight and assist with personal care. As a nurse, acknowledge and work with family members respectfully; they are partners in care, not obstacles. Additionally, patient modesty (particularly around the body) is paramount in Latin American and Spanish cultures; always ask permission, drape appropriately, and explain procedures before touching. Finally, formality in address is crucial: use 'usted' and the patient's surname until invited to do otherwise. Patients expect a certain professional distance in clinical settings, and informality can be misinterpreted as lack of respect or competence. When teaching about medications and side effects, be direct and honest; patients appreciate clarity over soft language, and they will share concerns more readily if you establish trust through straightforward, respectful communication.
